label: Sarajevo
sub: Site of the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand on June 28, 1914, triggering the July Crisis and the outbreak of World War I.

label: Paris
sub: French capital and strategic Allied hub. German forces came within 30 miles in 1914 before being halted at the Marne. Hosted the 1919 Peace Conference.

label: London
sub: British capital, center of the British Empire's war effort. Site of major voluntary recruiting drives in 1914 and later bombing by Zeppelin airships.

label: Berlin
sub: German Imperial capital and seat of the Kaiser. Scene of revolutionary upheaval in November 1918 that led to Wilhelm II's abdication.

label: Vienna
sub: Capital of the Austro-Hungarian Empire. Emperor Franz Joseph signed the declaration of war on Serbia on July 28, 1914, initiating the conflict.

label: Brussels
sub: Belgian capital occupied by Germany from August 20, 1914 until the Armistice. Seat of German military government in occupied Belgium.

label: Verdun
sub: Site of the longest battle of WWI (Feb–Dec 1916). Approximately 700,000 casualties. France's national symbol of resistance — 'Ils ne passeront pas.'

label: Ypres (Ieper)
sub: Focal point of four major battles (1914, 1915, 1917, 1918). The Ypres Salient was held by the Allies throughout the war at enormous cost. Menin Gate memorial stands here.

label: Battle of the Somme
sub: July–November 1916. Over 1.2 million total casualties. On July 1, 1916 alone, the British suffered 57,470 casualties — the bloodiest day in British military history.

label: Passchendaele
sub: Third Battle of Ypres (Jul–Nov 1917). British gained 8 km through seas of mud at the cost of 310,000 casualties. Symbol of futile trench warfare.

label: Vimy Ridge
sub: Captured by all four Canadian Corps divisions on April 9, 1917. Canada's defining national battle; a massive memorial now stands at the site.

label: Mons
sub: First major British engagement of WWI (Aug 23, 1914). Also the location where British forces received news of the Armistice on November 11, 1918.

label: Marne River
sub: Site of two decisive battles (1914, 1918). The First Battle of the Marne halted the German advance on Paris and ended the war of movement.

label: Amiens
sub: Battle of Amiens (Aug 8, 1918) launched the Hundred Days Offensive. Ludendorff called it the 'Black Day of the German Army.' Allied forces advanced 15 km on the first day.

label: Cambrai
sub: Battle of Cambrai (Nov 1917): first mass use of 476 tanks achieved a 7-km breakthrough. The model for 1918 combined-arms warfare.

label: Arras
sub: Battle of Arras (April 1917). British forces used the ancient tunnel network (Arras caves) for surprise. Canadians captured Vimy Ridge as part of this offensive.

label: Liège
sub: Belgian fortress city that resisted German advance for 12 days (Aug 4–16, 1914). Belgium's resistance slowed the Schlieffen Plan timetable.

label: Louvain (Leuven)
sub: German forces burned the medieval university city in August 1914, destroying 300,000 irreplaceable manuscripts. The 'Rape of Belgium' became a major Allied propaganda event.

label: Fort Douaumont
sub: Largest Verdun fortress, captured by Germany Feb 25, 1916, recaptured by France Oct 24, 1916. Its capture and loss defined the Verdun campaign.

label: Fort Vaux
sub: French fortress at Verdun. Heroically defended for seven days by a garrison of 600 French soldiers before falling to Germany on June 7, 1916.

label: Chemin des Dames
sub: Site of Nivelle's disastrous 1917 offensive. The failure triggered mutinies in 54 French divisions. Also site of the Third Battle of the Aisne (May 1918).

label: Beaumont-Hamel
sub: Newfoundland Regiment lost 91% casualties on July 1, 1916 (733 of 801 men). Newfoundland's Memorial Park preserves the original trenches.

label: Thiepval
sub: Ulster Division devastated July 1, 1916. The Thiepval Memorial commemorates 72,000 British and South African soldiers with no known grave on the Somme.

label: Pozières
sub: Australian divisions suffered 23,000 casualties fighting for this village on the Somme between July and September 1916—among the highest Australian casualty rates of the war.

label: Messines Ridge
sub: Battle of Messines (June 7, 1917): British detonated 19 massive mines under German lines, the largest non-nuclear explosion in history to that point, killing 10,000 Germans instantly.

label: Forest of Compiègne
sub: Site where the Armistice was signed in Foch's railway carriage on November 11, 1918. Hitler chose the same carriage for France's 1940 armistice as a deliberate humiliation.

label: Versailles
sub: The Hall of Mirrors witnessed Germany's unification in 1871 and Germany's humiliation in the 1919 peace treaty. The Treaty was signed June 28, 1919.

label: Antwerp
sub: Belgian fortress city fell to German siege artillery on October 10, 1914. Churchill personally went to Antwerp to bolster the defense, unsuccessfully.

label: ANZAC Cove
sub: Landing beach of Australian and New Zealand forces on April 25, 1915. Held under constant Turkish fire for eight months. April 25 is now ANZAC Day.

label: Cape Helles
sub: British landing site at the southern tip of Gallipoli, April 25, 1915. V Beach witnessed catastrophic casualties as troops left River Clyde under Turkish machine gun fire.

label: Suvla Bay
sub: Site of failed August 1915 Allied landings intended to break the Gallipoli deadlock. British forces failed to advance from the beach despite initial lack of opposition.

label: Dardanelles Strait
sub: Strategic waterway linking the Mediterranean to the Black Sea. Allied naval attack of March 18, 1915 failed to force the strait, losing three battleships to mines.

label: Scapa Flow
sub: Main base of the British Grand Fleet throughout the war. In June 1919, interned German fleet was scuttled here by Admiral von Reuter rather than surrender to the British.

label: Battle of Jutland
sub: The largest naval battle in history (May 31–June 1, 1916). 250 ships, 100,000 men. Britain lost 14 ships; Germany 11. Germany never challenged British naval supremacy again.

label: Kiel
sub: Main German naval base. Site of the November 1918 naval mutiny that sparked the German Revolution, leading to the Kaiser's abdication.

label: Zeebrugge
sub: German-held Belgian port used as a U-boat base. Scene of the famous Zeebrugge Raid (April 23, 1918) in which the British attempted to block the harbor entrance.

label: Dogger Bank
sub: Naval battle (January 24, 1915): British battlecruisers caught German forces, sinking the armored cruiser SMS Blücher. Germany's last significant offensive naval action until Jutland.

label: Tannenberg
sub: Germany encircled and destroyed the Russian Second Army (August 26–30, 1914), capturing 92,000 prisoners. General Samsonov shot himself. Hindenburg and Ludendorff became German heroes.

label: Masurian Lakes
sub: German victories over Russian First Army in September 1914 and again in February 1915 (Winter Battle in Masuria), eliminating the Russian threat to East Prussia.

label: Warsaw
sub: Russian-held capital of the Congress Kingdom of Poland. Captured by Germany and Austria in August 1915 during the Great Retreat, forcing Russian armies east.

label: Brest-Litovsk
sub: Site where Russia signed the harsh Treaty of Brest-Litovsk (March 3, 1918), ceding a third of European Russia and ending the Eastern Front.

label: Lemberg (Lviv)
sub: Major Austro-Hungarian city, scene of intense fighting in 1914. Captured by Russia in September 1914, recaptured by Austria-Germany in June 1915. Changed hands multiple times.

label: Przemyśl
sub: Major Austro-Hungarian fortress besieged by Russia for 133 days. 117,000-man garrison surrendered March 22, 1915. Recaptured by Austria-Germany June 1915.

label: Petrograd (St. Petersburg)
sub: Russian capital (renamed from St. Petersburg in 1914). Site of the February and October 1917 Revolutions. Bolsheviks took power here on November 7, 1917.

label: Salonika (Thessaloniki)
sub: Allied base in neutral Greece from 1915. The 'Gardeners of Salonika' maintained a front in Macedonia. The September 1918 offensive here broke Bulgaria and opened the Balkans.

label: Sofia
sub: Bulgarian capital. Bulgaria joined the Central Powers in October 1915 to seize Serbian Macedonia. Bulgaria's armistice (Sept 29, 1918) was the first crack in the Central Powers' collapse.

label: Constantinople (Istanbul)
sub: Ottoman capital. Churchill's Gallipoli campaign aimed to capture Constantinople and knock the Ottomans out of the war. Allied warships entered the city after the armistice of Mudros.

label: Jerusalem
sub: British General Allenby captured Jerusalem on December 9, 1917, a major propaganda victory. Allenby entered on foot to show respect, unlike Kaiser Wilhelm who had entered on horseback in 1898.

label: Damascus
sub: Captured by Allenby's forces and Arab irregulars on October 1, 1918. T.E. Lawrence (Lawrence of Arabia) entered with the Arab forces. Became Syria's capital under French mandate.

label: Gaza
sub: Three battles of Gaza (March–October 1917). The first two were British failures; the Third Battle finally broke through the Ottoman line after ANZAC cavalry took Beersheba.

label: Rome
sub: Italian capital. Italy signed the secret Treaty of London (April 1915) here, pledging to join the Allies in exchange for promised territorial gains.

label: Caporetto (Kobarid)
sub: Italian disaster (Oct 24, 1917): Austro-German stormtroopers routed the Italian Army, capturing 275,000 prisoners. Italy retreated 150 km. Fictionalized in Hemingway's A Farewell to Arms.

label: Isonzo River
sub: Scene of eleven Italian offensives against Austria-Hungary between 1915 and 1917. Italy gained little ground at enormous cost before the catastrophe of Caporetto.

label: Athens
sub: Greece was divided between pro-Allied Prime Minister Venizelos and neutral King Constantine I. The Allied threat to bombard Athens in December 1916 forced Greek compliance.

label: Dublin — Easter Rising
sub: Irish Republicans seized key buildings April 24, 1916, proclaiming the Irish Republic. British suppression and executions of 15 leaders transformed Irish political opinion toward independence.

label: St. Mihiel
sub: First all-American offensive (September 12–16, 1918): AEF under Pershing eliminated the St. Mihiel salient, capturing 13,000 prisoners. America's first independent operation of the war.

label: Meuse-Argonne
sub: America's largest battle: 1.2 million AEF soldiers attacked September 26, 1918. 122,000 US casualties. Sergeant Alvin York's single-handed capture of 132 prisoners occurred here.

label: Lutsk — Brusilov Offensive
sub: Key breakthrough point of the Brusilov Offensive (June 4, 1916). The most successful Allied offensive of WWI, capturing 400,000 prisoners and destroying Austro-Hungarian combat capacity.

label: Bucharest
sub: Romanian capital, occupied by German forces on December 6, 1916 after Romania's disastrous entry into the war. Romania's oil fields became vital to Germany's war effort.

label: Mecca — Arab Revolt
sub: Sharif Hussein ibn Ali launched the Arab Revolt from Mecca on June 5, 1916, supported by British gold and T.E. Lawrence. Aimed to expel Ottomans and create an independent Arab state.

label: Aqaba
sub: T.E. Lawrence and Arab irregular forces captured the port of Aqaba on July 6, 1917 by attacking overland from the north, in a daring operation that secured a vital supply base.

label: Doullens
sub: Allied leaders met here on March 26, 1918 during the German Spring Offensive to appoint Foch as Supreme Allied Commander—the key decision that unified Allied strategy.

label: Hindenburg Line
sub: Germany's main defensive position from early 1917, a 160-km line of fortifications withdrawing to which shortened German lines. Broken by Allied assault September–October 1918.

label: High Wood (Somme)
sub: British and Scottish troops fought for this wood on the Somme for six weeks between July and September 1916, suffering over 8,000 casualties before finally capturing it.

label: Delville Wood (Devil's Wood)
sub: South African Brigade held this wood on the Somme for a week in July 1916, suffering 2,536 casualties from 3,153 men—among the highest proportional losses of any Commonwealth force.

label: Langemarck
sub: Site of Germany's first large-scale chlorine gas attack on April 22, 1915, and of the 1914 Kindermord—German student volunteers advancing in singing, singing formations, suffering catastrophic losses.

label: Hamel
sub: Battle of Hamel (July 4, 1918): General Monash's Australian Corps took this village in 93 minutes using carefully rehearsed combined-arms tactics, a model for the Hundred Days.

label: Gorlice-Tarnów
sub: German-Austro breakthrough (May 2, 1915) using concentrated artillery, shattering Russian lines and initiating the Great Retreat that cost Russia all of Poland by September 1915.

label: Belgrade
sub: Serbian capital bombarded by Austro-Hungary from July 29, 1914. Serbia resisted three invasions in 1914 before being finally overrun when Bulgaria joined in October 1915.

label: Corfu
sub: Serbian army evacuated here by French Navy after catastrophic winter retreat through Albania in 1915–1916. 150,000 survivors were eventually transferred to the Salonika front.

label: Beersheba
sub: Australian Light Horse charged and captured the wells of Beersheba on October 31, 1917 in the last successful cavalry charge in history, breaking the Ottoman line in Palestine.

label: Spa, Belgium — German HQ
sub: German Supreme Headquarters (OHL) from 1917. Kaiser Wilhelm II and Ludendorff directed operations from here. It was at Spa that the Kaiser was persuaded to abdicate in November 1918.

label: Munich
sub: A socialist Bavarian Republic was proclaimed here on November 7, 1918, two days before the Kaiser's abdication. A young Adolf Hitler, recovering from mustard gas wounds, heard of the Armistice in a military hospital near Munich.

label: Voie Sacrée (Sacred Way)
sub: The only road supplying Verdun, along which 6,000 trucks carried 25 million men and 4 million tonnes of supplies in 1916. France's lifeline during the Battle of Verdun.

label: Paris — Bombardment by Paris Gun
sub: Germany's 'Paris Gun' (Wilhelmgeschütz) bombarded Paris from 120 km range beginning March 23, 1918. The shells killed 256 people; the gun's existence terrified civilians.

label: Menin Gate, Ypres
sub: Memorial to 54,896 Commonwealth soldiers with no known grave who died in the Ypres Salient. The Last Post has been sounded here every evening since 1928, except during WWII German occupation.

label: Thiepval Memorial
sub: Largest British war memorial in the world, bearing 72,000 names of soldiers missing on the Somme. Designed by Edwin Lutyens, unveiled 1932.

label: Canadian National Vimy Memorial
sub: Monumental memorial to 66,655 Canadians who died in WWI, particularly commemorating those with no known grave. Designed by Walter Allward, inaugurated 1936.

label: Abbeville — Allied War Council
sub: Key Allied logistics and command center. Site of May 1918 Abbeville Conference where Wilson and Foch debated the independent use of American forces versus feeding them into Allied units.

label: Lys River — Operation Georgette
sub: Germany's second 1918 Spring Offensive (Operation Georgette, April 9–29) struck the Lys River front, threatening Ypres and Channel ports. Haig issued his 'backs to the wall' order.
